stem: In a game, a fair six-sided die is rolled once. If the result is at least [[k]], you win \( [[w]] \) dollars; otherwise you lose \( [[l]] \) dollars. What is the expected value of your payoff, in dollars?
solution: There are \( 7 - [[k]] \) winning faces and \( [[k]] - 1 \) losing faces out of 6. The expected payoff is \( \frac{7 - [[k]]}{6}([[w]]) + \frac{[[k]] - 1}{6}(-[[l]]) = [[answer]] \) dollars.
distractor_rationales: This counts only the expected winnings and ignores the possibility of losing.
This gives only the expected loss magnitude, ignoring the winnings.
This adds the expected loss instead of subtracting it (sign error on the loss).
This ignores the probabilities and just subtracts the stakes.
This swaps which outcome each probability multiplies.
This is off by one dollar from the correct expected value.
This is off by one dollar from the correct expected value.

stem: In a game, a fair six-sided die is rolled once. If the result is at least [[k]], you win \( [[w]] \) dollars; otherwise you must pay a penalty of \( x \) dollars. What value of \( x \) makes this a fair game (expected payoff equal to \( 0 \))?
solution: Setting expected payoff to zero: \( \frac{7-[[k]]}{6}[[w]] = \frac{[[k]]-1}{6}x \), so \( x = \frac{7-[[k]]}{[[k]]-1}[[w]] = [[answer]] \) dollars.
distractor_rationales: This inverts the ratio of winning to losing faces.
This just repeats the win amount, ignoring the different number of winning and losing faces.
This uses the total number of faces instead of the number of winning faces in the ratio.
This is the expected winnings term alone, not the penalty that balances it.
This has the correct ratio but the wrong sign.
This uses the losing-face ratio instead of the winning-face ratio.

stem: A fair six-sided die is rolled once. If the result is among the first [[n1]] faces, you win \( [[w1]] \) dollars. If it is among the next [[n2]] faces, your payoff is \( [[w2]] \) dollars. Otherwise (the remaining faces), your payoff is \( [[w3]] \) dollars. What is the expected value of your payoff, in dollars?
solution: Weight each payoff by the probability of its outcome group: \( E = \frac{[[n1]]}{6}([[w1]]) + \frac{[[n2]]}{6}([[w2]]) + \frac{6-[[n1]]-[[n2]]}{6}([[w3]]) = [[answer]] \) dollars.
distractor_rationales: This drops the third outcome group entirely, ignoring its payoff and probability.
This naively averages the three payoffs as if each outcome group had equal probability \( \tfrac{1}{3}\), ignoring the actual face counts.
This flips the sign on the second and third terms instead of adding all three weighted payoffs.
This swaps the probability weights assigned to the first and third outcome groups.
This just adds the three raw payoffs without weighting by their probabilities at all.
This merges the first two groups' probabilities but drops their payoffs' distinct weighting.

stem: An urn contains [[r]] red balls and [[b]] blue balls. You draw one ball at random. If it is red, you win \( [[w]] \) dollars; if it is blue, you lose \( [[l]] \) dollars. What is the expected value of your payoff, in dollars?
solution: The probability of drawing red is \( \frac{[[r]]}{[[r]]+[[b]]} \) and of blue is \( \frac{[[b]]}{[[r]]+[[b]]} \). The expected payoff is \( \frac{[[r]]}{[[r]]+[[b]]}([[w]]) - \frac{[[b]]}{[[r]]+[[b]]}([[l]]) = [[answer]] \) dollars.
distractor_rationales: This counts only the expected winnings and ignores the possibility of drawing blue.
This gives only the expected loss magnitude, ignoring the winnings.
This adds the expected loss instead of subtracting it (sign error on the loss).
This swaps which color's probability multiplies which payoff.
This uses the odds ratio \( r/b \) instead of the probability \( r/(r+b) \).
This ignores the probabilities entirely and just subtracts the stakes.
